case_name: Mohammed Aynuddin @ Miyam vs State Of Andhra Pradesh
facts: On 17.12.1993 the appellant, a driver of an Andhra Pradesh Road Transport Corporation bus, moved the bus forward when a passenger, Agamma, was boarding it enroute. Agamma fell out of the vehicle and the rear wheel ran over her. She died of the injuries sustained. The driver was convicted by the trial court under Section 304A IPC, and the conviction was affirmed by the Sessions Court and the High Court in revision, leading to the appeal to the Supreme Court.
arguments_claimant: The appellant challenged the conclusion that he was guilty of negligent driving merely because the passenger fell while boarding. He contended that there was insufficient evidence that he started the bus suddenly before she could board or that he moved the bus without a conductor’s signal, and therefore criminal negligence could not be presumed on the scant evidence.
ratio_decidendi: The Court held that "It is a wrong proposition that for any motor accident negligence of the driver should be presumed" and that "Merely because a passenger fell down from the bus while boarding the bus no presumption of negligence can be drawn against the driver of the bus." It explained that the principle of "res ipsa loquitor is only a rule of evidence" and applies only when the nature of the accident and circumstances reasonably indicate the accident would not have occurred "in the absence of negligence" and the injuring thing was under the control of the alleged wrongdoer. On the proved facts, "no witness has said, including the conductor, that the driver moved the vehicle before getting signal to move forward" and the "evidence in this case is too scanty to fasten him with criminal negligence." As "Such further evidence is lacking in this case," the Court concluded it was "disabled from concluding that the victim fell down only because of the negligent driving of the bus," rendering the conviction under Section 304A IPC "unsustainable" and requiring acquittal.
final_order: The appeal was allowed; the conviction and sentence under Section 304A IPC were set aside and the appellant was acquitted.
summary: This Supreme Court criminal appeal arose from a bus passenger’s death when she fell while boarding and was run over. Although the lower courts convicted the driver for causing death by negligence, the Supreme Court held that negligence cannot be presumed merely from the accident and that the evidence did not show the driver moved the bus without signal or suddenly while boarding was ongoing. The Court set aside the conviction and acquitted the driver.
embedding_summary: Mohammed Aynuddin @ Miyam v. State of Andhra Pradesh, Supreme Court of India, decided 28/07/2000 (AIR 2000 SC 2511; (2000) 7 SCC 72). Accident on 17.12.1993 involving an Andhra Pradesh Road Transport Corporation bus: passenger Agamma fell while boarding as the bus moved forward; rear wheel ran over her causing death; driver convicted under Section 304A IPC (three months imprisonment) by trial court, affirmed by Sessions Court and High Court in revision. Core issue: whether negligent/rash driving can be inferred or presumed from the mere fact of a passenger falling while boarding, and applicability of res ipsa loquitur in criminal negligence. Supreme Court held no automatic presumption of negligence in motor accidents; res ipsa loquitur is only a rule of evidence shifting onus when circumstances show accident would not occur without negligence and instrumentality was under defendant’s control. Since no witness (including conductor) stated driver moved before signal and evidence was scanty, conviction under Section 304A IPC was unsustainable; appeal allowed and driver acquitted.
legal_principles: No presumption of driver negligence merely because a motor accident occurred
Passenger falling while boarding a bus does not by itself raise a presumption of driver negligence
Res ipsa loquitur is a rule of evidence to determine onus of proof and applies only when accident would not occur without negligence and instrumentality was under defendant’s control
Criminal negligence requires failure to exercise reasonable and proper care and precaution; scant evidence cannot sustain conviction under Section 304A IPC
To fasten negligence on bus driver in boarding accidents, evidence is needed that bus was moved suddenly before passenger boarded or before conductor’s signal

Agamma fell out of the vehicle and the rear wheel ran over her. She died of the injuries sustained. The driver was convicted by the trial court under Section 304A IPC, and the conviction was affirmed by the Sessions Court and the High Court in revision, leading to the appeal to the Supreme Court. The appellant challenged the conclusion that he was guilty of negligent driving merely because the passenger fell while boarding. He contended that there was insufficient evidence that he started the bus suddenly before she could board or that he moved the bus without a conductor’s signal, and therefore criminal negligence could not be presumed on the scant evidence. The Court held that "It is a wrong proposition that for any motor accident negligence of the driver should be presumed" and that "Merely because a passenger fell down from the bus while boarding the bus no presumption of negligence can be drawn against the driver of the bus." It explained that the principle of "res ipsa loquitor is only a rule of evidence" and applies only when the nature of the accident and circumstances reasonably indicate the accident would not have occurred "in the absence of negligence" and the injuring thing was under the control of the alleged wrongdoer. On the proved facts, "no witness has said, including the conductor, that the driver moved the vehicle before getting signal to move forward" and the "evidence in this case is too scanty to fasten him with criminal negligence." As "Such further evidence is lacking in this case," the Court concluded it was "disabled from concluding that the victim fell down only because of the negligent driving of the bus," rendering the conviction under Section 304A IPC "unsustainable" and requiring acquittal. The appeal was allowed; the conviction and sentence under Section 304A IPC were set aside and the appellant was acquitted. Core issue: whether negligent/rash driving can be inferred or presumed from the mere fact of a passenger falling while boarding, and applicability of res ipsa loquitur in criminal negligence. Supreme Court held no automatic presumption of negligence in motor accidents; res ipsa loquitur is only a rule of evidence shifting onus when circumstances show accident would not occur without negligence and instrumentality was under defendant’s control.
